body.u: I left school at the age of 18, in the Summer of 1978. I thoughthought my whole life would change and to my surprize it didn’t really.

For the first 3 months after I had left school all I did was sit around the house and got into trouble because I was so lazy and I managed to get in mum’s bad books because I just didn’t want to do anything any more.
“Why don’t you get a job and stop moppingmoping under my feet”, mum told me after I had an argument with her over cleaning up my bedroom.

I felt as though I had just jumped striaghtstraight out of the fat into the fire. I used to think from a very early age how nice it would be to lay in bed until late morning and please myself for the rest of the day. But now I had done it, it just seemed so boreingboring that I wish I had stayed at school for yet another year now.
Then one morning I was in town doing the shopping for mum as there wasn’t much else to do. When in the middle of Liptons I met Gale. I hadn’t seen Gale since she joined the Police.
She smiled at me and we began to talk. She really did like her job and ther I really wished I had one. So I decided there and then to get one.

So I said goodbye to Gale and walked striaghtstraight down to the job centre. I was to join the Army although at that time I didn’t know it. If I had I would have put foot right down and I know I would have walked out and that would have been that.
When I got there the people seemed really nice. I had an interview with a very nice lady, after I had filled in a form to say what my interests were and how many exams I had.
She asked me all about my mum and dad and if they had ever worked, and weatherwhether both of them worked.
When I left the room I felt it had all been a waste of time. I wouldn’t get a job this way.
They had told me there was a vaccancevacancy in Woolworths. But that was only part time. There was also one in the hospital but that was an office job and I really wanted to get out and meet people.

Then my brother came home about 6 months after I had gone to job centre. To my surprise he gave me a signing up form. I laughtlaughed at him and said dont be silly I wouldn't be a Pongo if it was the last thing on earth. He said it was only a joke he said I popedpopped the form in my bedroom drawdrawer and forgot all about it until one day my best friend had signed up in the Horse Artilery.
From the moment she said it I know that was the job for me. I filled in the form and sent it off.
I received a lovely letter in return saying that I would be able to join with the next lot of recrutesrecruits which was in a month’s time.
I rushed down stairs to tell mum and das and they were really pleased for me.
OnOne month went by very quickly and I was soon packing my bags to go to Gablicesdale.

I hated every minute I spent there but it was only 3 months although to me it could have been three years. We had to do all the mucky things like cleaning out the loos and mucking out the stables. ScrubingScrubbing floors I was told that they always do this first to see if you really want to join,

In fact only 3 girls got in. I stuck it for the 3 months. Then I was transferred to AlbershotAlershot. I really started to enjoy myself. I used to come ofoff the drilling field with saddle sores all over my backside. But I loved riding so it didn’t really bother me.
Then came the day when I was made Sargent of the corps I was really pleased that I wrote home striaghtstraight away.
I came home on holiday soon after that anand mum and dad held a party for me.
When I managed to get back I found out I had been transferedtransferred right up to Scotland. I wrote home to tell them I wouldn’t be home for two years.
Within 4 weeks of being sent up to Scotland I managed to come ofoff a bolting horse on the drilling field and broke my leg. I had 8 weeks off and was sent home for 6 of them.
That was went I met Alain. He worked at the hospital. He was really nice. His trade was a plasterplasterer.
He often made joked about this. Then came the time when I had to go back to Scotland but to my surprise my prays had been answered and I had been transferedtransferred to Chamber.
From that day on I used to see Alain alota lot. One night we had been to one of the Armies disco’s and he asked me to marry him I said of course I would.
I had only one year left to serve in the Army and I dedcidedecided to leave after this. So we gogot engaged and a year went by really quick, I was really surprised.
Before I knew it I was married and heading towards Italy on a honeymoon.

We waited three years before we had any children. On September 3rd I had a little girl called Lyn. She was a really beautiful baby. 2 years later we had another one this time a little boy his name was John.
